steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The team's conclusion is that the amount of prior language exposure may influence a dog's ability to distinguish familiar from unfamiliar languages. Because all dogs were raised in similar settings, older dogs have had more cumulative exposure. A supporting finding must show that older dogs distinguish familiar from unfamiliar language better — not just that they respond to their familiar language, but that the gap between familiar and unfamiliar responses is larger for them.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• "Dogs showed a different pattern of brain activity when hearing the language they were accustomed to than when hearing the scrambled recording, and the difference in brain activity increased with the age of the dog scanned.": This compares familiar language to the scrambled recording, not to an unfamiliar language. The conclusion is about distinguishing familiar from unfamiliar language, so the wrong comparison is being made. ✗
  • "Although the dogs' general hearing sensitivity declined with age, dogs of all ages showed more brain activity in response to hearing the language they were accustomed to than in response to hearing the other language.": This shows all ages can distinguish the languages, but does not show that older dogs distinguish them better. The age-based difference in discrimination ability is absent. ✗
  • "The similarity between the pattern of brain activity a dog showed in response to hearing the scrambled recording and the pattern of brain activity it showed in response to hearing the language it was not accustomed to was greatest among older dogs.": This says older dogs treat the scrambled recording and the unfamiliar language more similarly — it does not show they distinguish familiar from unfamiliar better, and could suggest the opposite. ✗
  • "The difference between the pattern of brain activity a dog showed when hearing the language it was accustomed to and the pattern of brain activity it showed when hearing the language it was not accustomed to was greatest among older dogs.": A larger brain-activity difference between familiar and unfamiliar language in older dogs directly shows that more exposure (age as a proxy) strengthens the ability to distinguish the two. This is exactly what the conclusion predicts. ✓
That matches the correct answer — D is correct.
